query: Binarly Debian Docker Hub xz backdoor August 2025 report affected images

mako.raw:
{"query":"Binarly Debian Docker Hub xz backdoor August 2025 report affected images","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1384,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.binarly.io/reports/xz-backdoor","title":"XZ backdoor | Binarly","content":"In this blog, we share a new finding in the XZ Utils saga: several Docker images built around the time of the compromise contain the backdoor.
On March 29, right before Easter weekend, we received notifications about something unusual happening with the open-source project XZ Utils, which provides lossless data compression on virtually all Unix-like operating systems, including Linux."},{"url":"https://www.darkreading.com/vulnerabilities-threats/xz-utils-backdoor-live-old-docker-images","title":"XZ Utils Backdoor Lives on in Old Docker Images","content":"The backdoor was designated CVE-2024-3094 and given a CVSS score of 10 (the highest possible).
According to Binarly researchers, "several Docker images built around the time of the compromise contain the backdoor," and some are still available via the container image library Docker Hub.
Although its search for backdoors was not comprehensive, researchers identified 35 Debian images on Docker Hub that contained the backdoor.
That includes 12 Docker images and 23 second-order images.
"While this may seem like a small number, we only scanned a small portion of the images published on DockerHub, stopping at second-order images.
Binarly said it "only partially" agrees with the response, stating that "leaving publicly available Docker images that contain a potential network-reachable backdoor poses a significant security risk.""},{"url":"https://thehackernews.com/2025/08/researchers-spot-xz-utils-backdoor-in.html","title":"Researchers Spot XZ Utils Backdoor in Dozens of Docker Hub Images, Fueling Supply Chain Risks","content":"New research has uncovered Docker images on Docker Hub that contain the infamous XZ Utils backdoor, more than a year after the discovery of the incident.
The XZ Utils supply chain event (CVE-2024-3094, CVSS score: 10.0) came to light in late March 2024, when Andres Freund sounded the alarm on a backdoor embedded within XZ Utils versions 5.6.0 and 5.6.1.
Specifically, the backdoor – placed in the liblzma.so library and used by the OpenSSH server – was designed such that it triggered when a client interacts with the infected SSH server."},{"url":"https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/docker-hub-still-hosts-dozens-of-linux-images-with-the-xz-backdoor/","title":"Docker Hub still hosts dozens of Linux images with the XZ backdoor","content":"The XZ-Utils backdoor, first discovered in March 2024, is still present in at least 35 Linux images on Docker Hub, potentially putting users, organizations, and their data at risk.
Binarly researchers have discovered numerous Docker images still impacted by the XZ-Utils backdoor.
The XZ-Utils backdoor, tracked under CVE-2024-3094, was malicious code hidden in the liblzma.so library of the xz-utils compression tool, versions 5.6.0 and 5.6.1.
It hooked the RSA_public_decrypt function in OpenSSH via glibc's IFUNC mechanism, so if an attacker with a special private key connected over SSH to an affected system, they could bypass authentication and remotely run commands as root.
The backdoor was stealthily injected by a long-time project contributor named "Jia Tan," and shipped in official Linux distro packages like Debian, Fedora, OpenSUSE, and Red Hat, making it one of the most severe software supply chain compromises last year.
"We identified more than 35 images that ship with the backdoor," explains Binarly in its report.
The same applies to all images that may contain a compromised version of the XZ-Utils backdoor, so users should manually check and ensure the library is on version 5.6.2 or later (the latest stable is 5.8.1)."}]}

Developers maintaining the images made the \"intentional choice\" to leave the artifacts available as \"a historical curiosity,\" given the improbability they'd be exploited. Binarly on Aug. 12 published research concerning the XY Utils backdoor, a notorious incident in which a developer known as \"Jia Tan\" spent two years building trust and credibility developing for open source lossless data compressor XZ Utils, only to then build a malicious Linux backdoor into the project. According to Binarly researchers, \"several Docker images built around the time of the compromise contain the backdoor,\" and some are still available via the container image library Docker Hub. Although its search for backdoors was not comprehensive, researchers identified 35 Debian images on Docker Hub that contained the backdoor. \"The impact on Docker images from Fedora, OpenSUSE, and other distributions that were impacted by the XZ Utils backdoor remains unknown at this time.\".","score":0.7982658,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.secureblink.com/threat-feeds/35-docker-hub-images-still-carry-the-deadly-xz-backdoor-after-1-year","title":"35 Docker Hub Images Still Carry the Deadly XZ Backdoor After 1 Year","content":"A highly sophisticated backdoor (CVE‑2024‑3094) was discovered in the Linux XZ‑Utils compression library—specifically in the `liblzma.so`","score":0.61112726,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.binarly.io/blog/persistent-risk-xz-utils-backdoor-still-lurking-docker","title":"Persistent Risk: XZ Utils Backdoor Still Lurking in Docker Images | Binarly","content":"## Get started today, Ship and Buy Software You Can Prove Is Safe.
